title: Lamb samosa
subtitle: Spiced minced lamb in crisp pastry
excerpt: Crisp triangular pastry cases filled with a dry, spiced minced lamb mixture with peas and warming spices. The lamb filling uses the same technique as keema matar (cooked until completely dry) which is essential for keeping the pastry crisp. A richer, meatier starter than the vegetable version, ordered alongside or instead of onion bhaji at the curry-house table.
sourceNotes: Lamb samosa follows the same pastry technique as vegetable samosa with a spiced minced meat filling — a format found across South Asia and the Middle East. The British curry-house version uses the same unleavened, oil-enriched pastry as the vegetable samosa.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze uncooked samosas on a tray, then bag. Fry from frozen, adding 3 to 4 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Make in large batches and freeze uncooked.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Fill and seal samosas up to a day ahead; refrigerate uncovered.

content.text: The lamb filling must be completely dry before it goes into the pastry. Moisture from the mince will steam inside the sealed pastry and make the crust soft rather than crisp. After cooking, spread the filling on a plate and leave it for 15 minutes so any residual steam escapes and the surface dries further. Only fill the samosas when the mixture is at room temperature or cooler.

The dough is the same as the vegetable samosa — oil-enriched plain flour with enough water to form a firm, pliable dough that rolls thin without tearing. The technique for shaping is identical: cone from a half-oval, fill, seal, and fry at 180°C. See the vegetable samosa entry for technique detail on the pastry.

items.prepNote: for the dough, plus 1 litre for frying

content.text: Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and 3 tablespoons {{vegetable-oil}} until it resembles breadcrumbs. Add cold water a tablespoon at a time (about 5 to 6 tablespoons) to form a firm dough. Knead 2 minutes, wrap, and rest 30 minutes. Meanwhile, heat 1 tablespoon of oil in a pan. Cook the {{onion}} for 5 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, and {{chilli-powder}} and stir for 30 seconds. Add the {{lamb-mince}} and cook for 8 minutes, breaking up, until completely browned and dry. Stir in the {{peas-frozen}}, {{garam-masala}}, and {{coriander}}. Season and cool completely.

Divide the dough into 4 pieces. Roll each into a thin oval, about 3 mm thick. Cut each in half. Dampen the straight edge of one half and form into a cone, pressing the seam firmly. Fill two-thirds full with cooled lamb mixture. Moisten the open edge and press to seal into a triangle. Repeat for remaining pieces. Heat 1 litre of {{vegetable-oil}} to 180°C. Fry 2 to 3 samosas at a time for 5 to 6 minutes, turning once, until golden and crisp. Drain on paper.

items.cause: Filling still warm when assembled, or oil too cool
items.fix: The filling must be at room temperature or cooler. Fry at exactly 180°C — test with a thermometer or a dough-drop test

items.cause: Seal not pressed firmly enough or filling too wet
items.fix: Press the seam firmly with dampened fingers. Ensure the filling is completely dry and cool before filling
Taste the lamb filling before it cools. It should be noticeably spiced and well-salted — pastry dampens flavour

items.symptom: Pastry puffs away from filling leaving air pockets
items.cause: Too little filling or too thick a pastry layer
items.fix: Roll the pastry thin (3 mm) and fill fully — two-thirds full is correct, not one-half

content.text: Lamb samosas are the meat counterpart to vegetable samosas at the British curry-house starter course. They share the same pastry technique but produce a denser, richer result. Making samosas in advance and freezing uncooked is particularly practical: they can be pulled from the freezer and fried directly, producing a fresher result than shop-bought. The lamb filling is also the keema matar filling — the two recipes share a base, so making one for dinner produces enough filling for the other as a side project.
